text:
The term ; alternatively spelt Ishbalan in the 2003 series and its related media, as well as in the Chuang Yi English manga; Ishbalite in the Trading Card Game; and Ishvarlan in some older VIZ manga translations; refers to the indigenous people of the nation called Ishval, which would come to be a region in the southeastern area of greater Amestris. The Ishvalans are an ethno-religious group of people identified by their ethnicity but unified by common religious beliefs and culture. 

Appearance

Ishvalans are characterised by three traits that nearly all share: dark brown skin, white hair, and red eyes. These seem to be dominant genetic traits that can show up regardless of whether or not Ishvalans marry outside of their ethnicity, as seen in the case of Miles. Despite only being 1/4 Ishvalan, he inherited all of the traits directly from his grandfather. Despite this, there is at least one known case of an Ishvalan lacking some of these traits, as an unnamed Ishvalan child (who is given the name Rick in the 2003 anime adaptation) is shown as having noticeably lighter skin than other Ishvalans. As Rick's character plays a very minor role in the manga, it is unknown if he is full Ishvalan or only partially Ishvalan and by how much. Because of this, one can only speculate if Rick is an indicator that full-blooded Ishvalans can have different traits, or if his differing traits are the cause of mixed ancestry.

The 2003 anime series embraces the idea that Ishvalans can vary in traits as while all have red eyes, the majority are depicted as having dark brown, black, or a dark yellowish-brown hair colour, with only a select few (such as Scar, the Ishvalan Elder, and the Ishvalan Exile) being shown to have white hair. They also all have brown skin, but the specific tone varies between each Ishvalan, with Rick himself once again having the lightest skin out of any Ishvalan, but now also having dark yellowish-brown hair. The 2003 anime also shows Rick's mother and brother Leo, who both have darker skin and darker brown hair than Rick. 

The 2009 anime series does the opposite, ensuring that all Ishvalans are portrayed with white hair, red eyes, and the same brown skin tone with no exceptions, meaning that even Rick is no longer unique and now has the same skin tone as all other Ishvalans.

Culture

The culture of the Ishvalan people is steeped heavily in their religious ideology and traditionalism, which marks the Ishvala as the sole true god of all creation. They are called to pray fervently to Ishvala in groups and live lives of simplicity and righteous piety as part of their religious creed, as it has been passed down through prophets like Logue Lowe. It is likely that the Ishvalans' strict code of religious conduct and discipline was borne from the severity of the land, which is not largely farmable and contains very few natural resources, if any. Ishvalans consider their names sacred, as gifts from God, and usually wear sashes striped with burgundy and gold, presumably as a sign of their faith. In accordance with their value of discipline, Ishvalan monks are also heavily trained in melee combat, presumably as a sort of meditative discipline, and are comparable on the battlefield to roughly ten standard soldiers. However, the most contentious point of the Ishvalan religion is its rejection of the craft of alchemy, considering it a practice based in sacrilegious hubris, perverting the natural creations of Ishvala into unnatural forms and thereby suggesting that human hands are more capable. This belief was later changed by Scar who now said said you have to use your mind and hands if your don’t doing it correctly it will be disastrous and therefore alchemy was never heresy to begin with. In the 2003 anime series, alchemy is so hated by the Ishvalan people that those among them found practicing it are exiled from their community and branded with a mark of shame that prohibits faithful Ishvalans from interacting with them.

History With Amestris

As a part of the annexation of Ishval, Ishvalans became normalized Amestrian citizens by law, allowing to practice their religion in keeping with Amestris' civil rights laws, able to participate in national practices as citizens as well as enlist in the State Military, but their radical cultural differences - particularly their aversion to alchemy - would put them at odds with the rest of the Amestrian population until tensions on both sides culminated in the Ishval Civil War in 1901. After seven years of constant fighting and stalemate conflicts, the Führer of Amestris ordered that the Ishvalan race be completely eradicated by a new force with State Alchemists at the vanguard. By the end of the bloody struggle, the Ishvalan people had nearly died out, with refugees scattering to slums all over the country as well as places outside the border, such as the ruins of Xerxes. As a result, Ishvalans have become a focus of discrimination, fear and pity among the greater Amestrian population and have fallen below even second-class citizen status.

At the end of the manga storyline, however, Ishvalan refugees are granted amnesty and allowed to return to their holy homeland. In addition, an active mission to re-establish Ishvalan culture is headed by Major Miles and Scar (possibly as the new Grand Cleric) under the command of Brigadier General Roy Mustang. At the end of the 2003 anime storyline, the Ishvalan people are mentioned by Sheska in a letter to Winry Rockbell during the series epilogue that after Amestris' government had reformed they allowed the Ishvalan people to return to their homeland, and are aiding them in rebuilding their homes.

Trivia

The Ishvalan culture appears to be based on an amalgamation of various different cultures including Middle Eastern and Indian cultures. The material culture and architecture is similar to the Middle East. The religious culture is based on Indian religions, with the deity Ishval similar the Hindu concept of Ishvara.
Hiromu Arakawa stated that the Ishvalans are partially based on the Ainu people, natives of Hokkaido, who were conquered and absorbed by the Japanese in the 19th and 20th centuries. https://doanimation.wordpress.com/2011/06/02/old-copypasta-of-hiromu-arakawa/ 
In the original storyline, most of the population of Amestris believe that all of the Ishvalans were killed during the civil war, save for the few people living in the various slums with them. However, in the first anime, one of the thugs who attacks the slums where Scar is recovering says "Aren't [the Ishvalans] supposed to be living in designated camps?", Fullmetal Alchemist (anime) Episode 25: Words of Farewell (2003 series) leading to the conclusion that not only are Amestrian citizens aware of surviving Ishvalans, the government is spending money to keep them in camps. This is further supported when the Ishvalan group that Scar was traveling with were eventually going to be sent to one of these camps Episode 36: The Sinner Within (2003 series) following their capture. Episode 32: Dante of the Deep Forest (2003 series) 
Many people believe the Ishvalans to be either an allusion to or a direct parallel of the Jewish people, due to the similarities in their respective plights; both are particular ethnoreligious groups who are, more often than not, a minority of the population of their residing country, while being subject to prejudice and discrimination against them, with attempts at genocide being made against the both of them. Just as Bradley issued for all Ishvalans to be exterminated, so did the Nazis seek to exterminate the Jewish population of Europe as the "Final Solution" to the "Jewish Question".  Both also had a homeland they were forcibly taken from, one Ishvalans began returning to by the end of the series, and Jews en masse to Israel with the Zionist movement. 
In the 2003 anime's movie Conqueror of Shamballa, direct comparisons are drawn between the Ishvalans and both the Jewish and Romani peoples, who were targeted during the Holocaust by Nazi Germany. The Ishvalans' desert homeland and their conservative, monotheistic faith have also drawn comparisons to to Judaism and Islam; in flashbacks, many Ishvalan women are seen wearing headresses which resemble Jewish headgear for women.
